name: Tuoni
description: Tuoni is the god of the underworld and death in Finnish mythology. He resides in Tuonela, the realm of the dead, which is often depicted as a dark and sorrowful place separated from the land of the living by a dark river. Tuoni is not necessarily malevolent but represents the inevitable fate of all mortals. He is the ruler of Tuonela, overseeing the souls of the departed. Unlike some death gods in other mythologies, Tuoni is not actively seeking to claim lives; rather, he maintains order within his realm and ensures the proper transition of souls. He is often associated with the concept of the grave and the final resting place. While not frequently invoked in prayers or rituals, Tuoni embodies the ultimate boundary of human existence and the acceptance of mortality within the Finnish worldview. The river of Tuonela is guarded by a fearsome swan or a boatman who ferries the souls of the dead across.
subtitle: God of the Underworld

keyMyths.title: The Journey to Tuonela
keyMyths.description: The journey to Tuonela is a perilous one, requiring the deceased to cross the dark river that separates the land of the living from the realm of the dead. Often, they are accompanied by a shaman or guide who can navigate the treacherous waters and negotiate with the guardians of the underworld. The journey symbolizes the transition from life to death and the acceptance of one's fate. The river itself represents the final boundary.

keyMyths.title: The Inhabitants of Tuonela
keyMyths.description: Tuonela is populated by the spirits of the dead, who exist in a shadowy and melancholic state. They are ruled by Tuoni and his wife, Tuonetar, and their children. The underworld is described as a place of darkness and silence, where the dead await their final fate. The inhabitants lack the vibrant energy of the living and are often depicted as pale and ethereal figures. They are not tortured or punished but simply exist in a state of perpetual rest.

keyMyths.title: Väinämöinen's Visit to Tuonela
keyMyths.description: In the Kalevala, Väinämöinen, the great Finnish hero, journeys to Tuonela to seek knowledge and wisdom from the dead. He travels across the river of Tuonela and encounters Tuoni and Tuonetar. However, he finds the experience unsettling and ultimately decides to return to the land of the living, realizing that the secrets of the dead are not meant for the living to possess. This myth highlights the boundary between the two worlds and the importance of respecting the natural order.
